Re: Request for Opinion
Dear si rs:
P1ea6e accept this as a reguest for written advice of the
Commisslon pur8uant to Government Coile S83114 (b) regarding the
foLIowing factual s cenari o.
The City of Monterey in j.ts Charter establishes a
Neighborhood. anil Community Improvement Program which is fundeCl
with 16* of the City's transient occupancy tax. 
The total funiled
budlget of this program is curlently about $1, 400,000, and i6 to
be spent only on capital projects of community-wide benefits
within the various neighborhoods of the City.
The Charter e6tabli6hes a Nelghborhood Improvement Program
Committee (hereinafter "Committee', ) comprlEeal of at least one
resident from each residential neighborhood in the City appointeal
by the City Council. The Comnittee recommenils a List of capital
improvements desireal to be accompli6hed in each neighborhood to
the City Counci.L. The CounclL aloes not have to fund each
project, but it may only expend this money on projects
recommend,ed. by this Committee.
The term " capital projects" as used in this program
includes, but is not Iimited to, streets, storm drains, 6evrera,
sidewalks, Iighting, traffic control devices, Iandscaping and
beautification, parks, recreatj.onal facil,ities, 
and public
bulldings. 
Obviously the j,nclusj,on of such improvements in a
neighborhood nray have a material financial effect on the value of
residentj.al property in recipient neighborhooals.
The City haE cleternined that the Committee in its
aleliberations ls subJect to the Ralph M. Brovrn Act as a
Iegislative body as that term is defined in Governnent code
^/--.-
',ONIERFY.
"r


554952.2. The City has also determined that the Committee
members must comply with our conflict of interest code and submit
annual FPPC Form 721 interest statements because of the direct
involvement in the making of clecisions which may foreseeably have
a material effect on the fj,nancial interest of the person taking
action pursuant to Government coale S87302.
Based on this information, are Commj-ttee representatives
disqualified from participating in the select.ion of proj ects for
their or.rn neighborhoocls? All representatives live within the
vicinity of neighborhood projects (certainly within 2500 feet,
often within 3OO feet), and many projects such as instaLlation of
sidewalks, construction of parks or neighborhood centers, or
installation of street lighting has a direct anal material effect
on property valuation although it is impossible to approximate
such values in the abstract in aclvance.
If representatives are disquaJ.ifiecl from voting for proj ects
in their own neighborhood, are they similarly disqualified from
projects outside their representative area, but within 2500 feet
of their resialence?
I have inclualed a copy of a recent opinion of this office
regarding applicability of the Brown Act and conflict provj-sions
to this committee as perhaps another source of information
regarding the operation of the Committee. I would be happy to
proviile any further information needeil to rencler your advice upon
re ques t.
Thank you in advance for your anticipated courtesy and
cooperati on.
william B. Conners
City Attorney

To:
CITY OF MONIEREY
Mayor and City Councilmembers; City Manager
From; 
City Attorney
Date: 
March 15, 1991
Subject: Neighborhood and Community Improvement Committee
It has been asked whether or not the Neighborhood and
Community Improvement Committee (herein NIP Committee) is an
official 
" Iegislatj-ve body" as that term is defined in the Ralph
M. Brown Act, and if so, is it subject to the terms and
conditions of that Act ?
A related question is whether or not the NIP Committee is
subject to the Political 
Reform Act of 1974 and thereby required
to submit conflict of interest forms.
It.is 
my opinion that the NIP Committee is a "Iegislative
body" and is therefore subject to the Brown Act, and members of
the NIP Committee should be required to comp.Lete and file
confl-ict of interest statements.
The Ralph M, Brown Act, in Government Code S54952.2 defines
"legislative body" as including any multimember body which
exercises any authority delegated to it by the primary
Iegislative body. In thj-s case, the NIP Committee is appointed
by the City council pursuant to Charter 56.6 d. The Committee .is
tasked with formulating a Iist of capital improvement projects
recommended to the Council for implementation. The city Council
then exercises its discretion in selecting, funding, and
constructing the improvements from the Ii-st.
It is clear that the NIP Committee is a multi.member body,
and that it acts in a legislative capacity in selecting
appropriate capital prol ects for possible implementation. while
the Charter limits the scope of their task, it is clear thai
their function is one of delegated responsibility from the City
Council. The Committee faffs squarely within the definltion
established in S54952.2, and therefore all meetings of that body
shall conform to the remaining provisions of the Brown Act.
The Political 
Reform Act of 1974 requires that local
agencies adopt conflict of interest cod.es to require l-oca}
officials 
who may be involved ln the making of decisj.ons $/hich
may foreseeabl-y have a material effect on a financj-al j-nterest of
the person taking action (covernment Code SB7302). Monterey has
aalopted such a code, and key City employees and all Board. and
Commission members are required to file a Fair political
Practices Commission (FPPC) forn each year setting forth
3 -r s'-.7 6


information rel-ated to potential conflicts of interest. 
At
present, members of the NIP Committee have not been inc.Iuded as
required to parti cipate.
The stated intent of this section is that aIl employees,
offlcers, 
and consultants \./hose positions entaj,l the makj-ng or
participatj.on in the making of decis j-ons which could foreseeably
have an impact on their own financial 
status must be included in
our conflict 
of interest 
code and file disclosure forms required
by the FPPC in a timety manner. As noted above, members of the
NIP Committee are appointed by the Council in much the same
manner as are members of Boards and. Commissions. They
participate 
in the decision to aLlocate signifj-cant 
sums of money
to various nej-ghborhood projects. 
It may be posslble, however
unl-j-kely, that a member cou.Id attempt to influence the decision
of whether or not to include a proj ect in the NIP program which
would have a financial 
i.mpact on the individual. 
For instance,
the member couLd vote to install 
sioevra.Iks ln various
neighborhoods while havj-ng a contract wj-th the City to anstall
aL] sidewalks required during a fiscal year. 
Other nore
reaList.ic examples could be presented as weII, but what is
important is that the statute attempts to require discLosure by
anyone who might have a conflict 
situation 
as a result of their
government position, 
and I think it is clear that this could
arise. 
Fo.r that reason, I bel-ieve our conffict 
of interest 
code
should be amended to include NIP Committee members, and they
should be required to fj-l-l- out appropriate EPPC forms.
It may be unfortunate that this body is reguired to comply
with these statutes, however, it does seem quite clear that the
laws cited do require participation. 
The City CIerk wil.l- be
asked to amend. the conflict 
of interest code, and the staff 
for
the Committee wifl be tasked with implementation of these two

Re: Letter No. 91-2 0 6
Dear Mr. Conners:
Your letter requesting advice under the Po1itical Reforn Act
was received on April L, L990 by the Fair Political Practj.ces
Commissi-on. rf you have any questi,ons about your advice request,
you may contact Mark Morodorni, an attorney in the Legal Division,
directly at (916) 322-590L.
we try to answer aIl, advice requests promptly, Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex lega1 questions, or
more infornation is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
to your request will, contact you shortly to advise you as to
information needed. If your request is for informa] assistance,
we wilt answer it as quickly as we can. (See Comnission
Regufation 18329 (2 Cal-. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)
You also should be aware that your ]etter and our response
are public records which rnay be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.
Very truly yours/,

sections.question: Based on this information, are Committee representatives disqualified from participating in the selection of projects for their own neighborhoods? All representatives live within the vicinity of neighborhood projects (certainly within 2500 feet, often within 300 feet), and many projects such as installation of sidewalks, construction of parks or neighborhood centers, or installation of street lighting has a direct and material effect on property valuation although it is impossible to approximate such values in the abstract in advance. If representatives are disqualified from voting for projects in their own neighborhood, are they similarly disqualified from projects outside their representative area, but within 2500 feet of their residence?
sections.question_synthetic: The City of Monterey seeks guidance on whether members of its Neighborhood Improvement Program Committee are disqualified from voting on capital projects in their own neighborhoods or within 2500 feet of their residences, given that such projects may materially affect property values and the members must file conflict of interest disclosures.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This document contains both an incoming request from City Attorney William B. Conners (dated March 29, 1991) and a prior city attorney opinion memo (dated March 15, 1991), followed by an FPPC acknowledgment letter (April 4, 1991) indicating receipt but not providing the actual advice. The advice letter itself is not included in this document. The FPPC acknowledgment references Letter No. 91-206.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Based on this information, are Committee representatives disqualified from participating in the selection of projects for their own neighborhoods? All representatives live within the vicinity of neighborhood projects (certainly within 2500 feet, often within 300 feet), and many projects such as installation of sidewalks, construction of parks or neighborhood centers, or installation of street lighting has a direct and material effect on property valuation although it is impossible to approximate such values in the abstract in advance. If representatives are disqualified from voting for projects in their own neighborhood, are they similarly disqualified from projects outside their representative area, but within 2500 feet of their residence?

embedding.summary: The City of Monterey, represented by its City Attorney, submitted a request for written advice from the California FPPC regarding conflict of interest disqualifications for members of the Neighborhood Improvement Program Committee, specifically whether they can vote on projects affecting their own neighborhoods. The FPPC acknowledged receipt of the request on April 4, 1991, and stated it would provide a response within 21 working days.
